extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r4 (priority 50), which requires all 10 conditions (g6pd_deficiency, hyperkalemia, seizure_history, elderly, diabetes, immunosuppressed, polypharmacy, prior_gi_bleed, hypertension, anticoagulated) to be true; otherwise it defaults to false. Thus rec_drug_a = true IFF all 10 conditions hold. For rec_drug_b: r2 (priority 50) sets it true when g6pd_deficiency holds, but r3 (priority 90) sets it false under exactly those same 10 conditions. In the only scenario where rec_drug_a is true (all 10 conditions), both r2 and r3 match for rec_drug_b, and the per-output priority resolution selects the highest-priority matching rule: r3 (90) beats r2 (50), forcing rec_drug_b = false. Therefore whenever rec_drug_a is true, rec_drug_b is necessarily false. No input can make (rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b) jointly true. The mutual-exclusion property holds across the entire input space.
